title: Shut It Down 2

jokes.text: Go blow-dry your face.
jokes.setup: Cab drivers complaining about safety issues and demanding action
jokes.punchline: Louie responds with a nonsensical insult instead of addressing their concerns
jokes.explanation: Perfect Louie dismissal - absurd, character-specific insult that shows his complete disregard for worker safety

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: You're all a bunch of... of... yarn-knitting, sock-darning, button-sewing morons!

jokes.text: How does that make you feel? / Horny as all get-out.
jokes.setup: Elaine calling out Louie's manipulation in moral terms
jokes.punchline: Louie's completely shameless and crude honesty
jokes.explanation: Excellent structure - moral setup completely deflated by Louie's shameless honesty. Perfect character moment.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: You know, Louie, what you do is immoral. You manipulate people, you take advantage of them.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Yeah? Well, let me tell you something - I do it because it works, and it makes me money. That's it. No philosophy, no excuses.

jokes.text: Haven't you ever heard of the Taft-Hartley Act? / Hey, I used to see them on The Ed Sullivan Show.
jokes.setup: Reference to labor law legislation
jokes.punchline: Tony thinks it's a comedy duo from variety TV
jokes.explanation: Classic Tony misunderstanding - shows his character while being genuinely clever wordplay

dialogue.speaker: Unknown
dialogue.line: You know about the labor law legislation?

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: Oh, you mean Abbott and Costello?

jokes.text: Taft was the goofy one, right?
jokes.setup: Continuing the Ed Sullivan Show misunderstanding
jokes.punchline: Tony now assigns comedy duo roles to historical figures
jokes.explanation: Extends the previous joke effectively but less impact as a follow-up

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: Okay, okay, I got it figured out. We're doing a comedy duo bit, right? So listen - I'm pairing up historical figures. Washington and Jefferson, they're the straight men. Benjamin Franklin, he's the funny one - always making cracks about electricity.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: Napoleon and Caesar, they got great timing together. One of them says something about conquering the world, the other one does a slow burn. The audience goes crazy.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: And for the big finale - Abraham Lincoln and George Washington. Lincoln does the setup about saving the Union, Washington delivers the punchline about wooden teeth. Kills 'em every time!

jokes.text: Well, today, she started in on mine.
jokes.setup: Explaining Elaine's nervous habit of biting her fingernails
jokes.punchline: Reveals she's so anxious she's now biting other people's nails
jokes.explanation: Excellent escalation joke - takes familiar nervous habit and pushes it to absurd extreme

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: She's so nervous she bites her fingernails.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Now she's so anxious she's biting other people's nails.

jokes.text: I mean, you could smell these things from home.
jokes.setup: Louie asking Jim to smell aftershaves because of his big nose
jokes.punchline: Alex commenting on Jim's supposedly powerful sense of smell
jokes.explanation: Standard physical feature comedy - functional but not particularly creative

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: With a nose like that, Jim, you could probably smell a steak being cooked in Staten Island.

jokes.text: Won't be a mosquito near him all night.
jokes.setup: Louie choosing 'Cruel Cavalier' aftershave
jokes.punchline: Suggesting it's so strong it works as insect repellent
jokes.explanation: Decent observational humor about cheap cologne being overpowering

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Cruel Cavalier? That's a great aftershave.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: Yeah, it's so strong, it works as insect repellent.

jokes.text: In a couple of hours, it's going to be ripped from my body.
jokes.setup: Louie complaining about pins in his new shirt
jokes.punchline: Revealing his delusional expectations for the date
jokes.explanation: Perfect Louie delusion - completely inappropriate confidence that creates great cringe comedy

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: These pins are killing me. Why do they put so many pins in a new shirt?

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: I'm taking Phyllis to dinner tonight. I want to look sharp.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: By the end of the evening, she's gonna be so impressed, she'll probably want to come back to my place.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: We'll have some wine, put on some music... maybe she'll want to stay the night.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: And in the morning, she'll make me breakfast in bed.

jokes.text: Nice to be back, Lou.
jokes.setup: Louie accidentally sticking Alex with a pin
jokes.punchline: Alex's understated sarcastic response to being injured
jokes.explanation: Good character moment for Alex - his dry response to Louie's carelessness

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: Oh, thank you, Louie. I've always wanted a flesh wound. It really completes my outfit.

jokes.text: You're early, and she doesn't live here, and I have a headache!
jokes.setup: Louie arriving for the date
jokes.punchline: Elaine's frantic triple-complaint revealing her panic
jokes.explanation: Great escalation structure - builds panic through three increasingly desperate statements

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Oh my God, he's here! My hair looks terrible, my outfit is all wrong, and I have nothing to say to him!

jokes.text: his name is Joe, and he's a tall blond doctor, and he likes me very much, and won't hurt me.
jokes.setup: Alex asking about Elaine's supposed other date
jokes.punchline: Elaine's obviously made-up description ending with the desperate 'won't hurt me'
jokes.explanation: Excellent character comedy - the 'won't hurt me' reveals her terror about Louie while trying to maintain the lie

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: So who is this other guy you're going out with?

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Oh, he's wonderful. He's a concert pianist. Very sensitive, very artistic.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: A concert pianist?

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Mm-hmm. He has long, delicate fingers. He speaks five languages. He's written poetry. He collects first editions.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: Elaine...

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: And most importantly... he won't hurt me.

jokes.text: Stubby Detmuller never did it.
jokes.setup: Alex saying shop stewards don't have to date Louie
jokes.punchline: Reference to previous shop steward who apparently also avoided this
jokes.explanation: Funny name and implication that dating Louie is somehow an expected shop steward duty.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: The last shop steward didn't have to date Louie either.

jokes.text: There was that one time when he went out dancing with Louie on New Year's Eve.
jokes.setup: Alex saying shop stewards don't have to date Louie, mentioning Stubby Detmuller
jokes.punchline: Revealing Stubby actually DID go dancing with Louie
jokes.explanation: Brilliant subversion - sets up the exception then immediately undercuts it with absurd precedent

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: Shop stewards don't have to date Louie. Look at Stubby Detmuller.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Stubby Detmuller? That bum went dancing with me last Tuesday night.

jokes.text: Open the door and feast your eyes on a 150 pounds of hickey bait!
jokes.setup: Louie arriving at Elaine's door
jokes.punchline: His completely inappropriate and crude self-description
jokes.explanation: Peak Louie grossness - 'hickey bait' is both specific and disgusting, perfectly character-integrated

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Hi there, sweetheart. It's me, Louie. I'm here to pick you up for our date. I'm a short, bald, obnoxious little weasel with a big mouth and no manners, but I got a heart of gold and a lotta cash.

jokes.text: Alex, if I ask you real nice, will you take this bottle and beat me senseless?
jokes.setup: Elaine hearing Louie's crude introduction
jokes.punchline: Her desperate request for unconsciousness as escape
jokes.explanation: Good escalation of Elaine's panic - the specific request for violence as preferable to the date

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Louie, could you please just hit me over the head with something?

jokes.text: You seem like vermin.
jokes.setup: Alex pretending to be Elaine's father, asking about Louie's profession
jokes.punchline: Blunt assessment after Louie says he's a dispatcher
jokes.explanation: Perfect economy and bluntness - 'vermin' is both accurate and unexpected from fake father figure

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: A dispatcher? What kind of job is that?

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: I tell cabs where to go.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: Well, you're a natural.

jokes.text: My intentions, Rieger, are to take a trip around the moon.
jokes.setup: Alex asking if Louie's intentions are honorable
jokes.punchline: Louie's crude metaphor for his sexual plans
jokes.explanation: Perfectly Louie response - crude but with his characteristic flowery language attempting to sound sophisticated

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: Louie, are your intentions honorable?

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Honorable? I'm gonna treat her like a new car — I'm gonna get in, start her up, and drive her around the block a few times.

jokes.text: There's not a man, woman, or child that we're going to bump into tonight who isn't on my payroll.
jokes.setup: Louie explaining his evening preparations
jokes.punchline: Revealing he's literally bribed everyone they'll encounter
jokes.explanation: Great character moment showing Louie's desperate manipulation and the absurd lengths he'll go to

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: You know what I do every night before I go out? I bribe everybody. The doorman, the hat check girl, the waiter, the busboy. Everybody gets a little something.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: That way, when I walk in anywhere, everybody knows me, everybody likes me, everybody treats me right.

jokes.text: Nardo, you look like Nanook of the North.
jokes.setup: Elaine appearing completely bundled up for the date
jokes.punchline: Louie's comparison to the famous arctic explorer
jokes.explanation: Good visual reference that clearly communicates how overdressed/protected Elaine is

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Where are you going, the North Pole? You look like Admiral Byrd!

jokes.text: It will take the coroner a week to pry the grin off my face.
jokes.setup: Alex threatening to kill Louie if he tries anything
jokes.punchline: Louie suggesting he'd die happy from the experience
jokes.explanation: Excellent dark comedy - vivid, specific image that perfectly captures Louie's delusional horniness

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: Louie, if you try anything with me, I'll kill you.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Yeah? Well, I'd die happy.

jokes.text: I'll just send the hounds out after the scent of 'Cruel Cavalier.'
jokes.setup: Louie saying Alex would have to catch him
jokes.punchline: Alex referencing the earlier aftershave scene
jokes.explanation: Good callback to earlier cologne joke, suggesting it's strong enough for tracking

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: You'd have to catch me first.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: That's okay. I've still got that aftershave you were wearing.

jokes.text: Tell them hi from Uncle Lou.
jokes.setup: Elaine asking to call her children
jokes.punchline: Louie's inappropriate insertion into her family
jokes.explanation: Good cringe moment showing Louie's presumption and inappropriate familiarity

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: I need to call my children. Can I use the phone?

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Sure, go ahead. But make it quick.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Thank you, Louie.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Tell them their Uncle Louie says hello.

jokes.text: I want you to always remember your mother the way she was the last time you saw her... Young and fresh and happy.
jokes.setup: Elaine calling her children before the date
jokes.punchline: Speaking as if she's going to her death
jokes.explanation: Brilliant escalation - treats a date like a death sentence, perfect melodrama that reveals her genuine terror

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Goodbye, children. I'm going on a date. I may not return.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: If I'm not back by midnight, divide my things among yourselves. The good china goes to Melissa.

jokes.text: The ice is broken.
jokes.setup: Louie presumably doing something awkward or inappropriate
jokes.punchline: His matter-of-fact assessment of the social situation
jokes.explanation: Decent understated humor - Louie's delusional confidence about social progress

dialogue.speaker: Louie De Palma
dialogue.line: What? I'm just being friendly. You got a problem with that?

jokes.text: You've written our entire conversation?
jokes.setup: Louie presenting written conversation notes
jokes.punchline: Elaine's disbelief at the absurd preparation
jokes.explanation: Good setup for the scripted conversation bit - Elaine's reasonable reaction to absurd situation

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: I've prepared some notes on how our conversation should go.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Notes? You wrote out notes for a conversation?

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: It helps me stay focused. See, here's where you laugh, and here's where I make my move.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: You've got it all planned out like a script?

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: I even marked the pauses for effect.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: This is insane.

jokes.text: 'Better is a poor and wise child than an old and foolish king.'
jokes.setup: Reading his scripted conversation about children
jokes.punchline: Quoting what sounds like biblical wisdom completely out of context
jokes.explanation: Funny character moment - Louie trying to sound profound with misapplied wisdom

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Listen to this. 'Children are a gift from God.' That's beautiful, ain't it? You know where that's from?

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: The instruction manual for my VCR.

jokes.text: Now, let's tear up this piece of paper and dance, stallion.
jokes.setup: Reading from his scripted conversation
jokes.punchline: His completely inappropriate pet name for Elaine
jokes.explanation: Peak Louie inappropriateness - 'stallion' is both absurd and revealing of his delusional self-image

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: His completely inappropriate pet name for Elaine

jokes.text: I spent all morning taking lessons from a gay Spanish guy.
jokes.setup: Claiming he can tango
jokes.punchline: His oddly specific description of his dance instructor
jokes.explanation: Decent character moment showing Louie's desperate preparation, though relies on stereotypes

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: I can tango. My instructor was a woman with a mole on her left shoulder blade, a slight limp in her right leg, and she always smelled like cabbage.

jokes.text: Play, savages!
jokes.setup: Demanding the band play tango music
jokes.punchline: His imperious and insulting command
jokes.explanation: Good Louie moment - shows his arrogance and complete lack of social awareness

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Play a tango!

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: And make it snappy, you bunch of has-beens!

jokes.text: To wash down the Valium.
jokes.setup: Louie noting Elaine had drinks before he arrived
jokes.punchline: Revealing she mixed alcohol with tranquilizers
jokes.explanation: Excellent dark comedy escalation - reveals how desperate she was to numb herself for the experience

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: You had drinks before I got here?

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: I mixed them with tranquilizers.

jokes.text: I thought something like that would kill a person. / So did I.
jokes.setup: Louie expressing concern about alcohol and Valium
jokes.punchline: Elaine revealing it was intentionally dangerous
jokes.explanation: Brilliant dark comedy - perfect economy, reveals Elaine preferred death to the date with Louie

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: You can't mix alcohol and Valium! That's dangerous!

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: That's exactly why I did it.

jokes.text: The least you could have done was strip down to your dress.
jokes.setup: Louie complaining about money spent on the date
jokes.punchline: His crude demand for her to remove protective clothing
jokes.explanation: Typical Louie grossness - shows his transactional view of the evening

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: You spent forty bucks on dinner? Forty bucks! Do you know what I could've done with forty bucks?

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Take off the dress.

jokes.text: All I wanted was to have one classy evening in my life
jokes.setup: Louie's emotional plea about being rough around the edges
jokes.punchline: Calling his crude behavior 'classy'
jokes.explanation: Great irony - Louie's complete lack of self-awareness about what constitutes 'classy' behavior

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: I may be rough around the edges, but I got class. I got sophistication.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: When I burp, I say 'excuse me.' When I pick my teeth, I use a toothpick instead of a matchstick. That's classy.

jokes.text: Enthusiasm like that doesn't come cheap.
jokes.setup: Elaine mentioning the dancing and crowd clapping
jokes.punchline: Revealing she knows the applause was bought
jokes.explanation: Good callback to Louie's earlier admission about paying everyone - shows Elaine caught on

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: They were clapping and cheering, it was amazing!

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: You know, I hired them.

jokes.text: The final kiss and Louie's reaction
jokes.setup: Elaine finally agreeing to give Louie a kiss
jokes.punchline: Whatever physical comedy occurs during or after the kiss
jokes.explanation: Physical comedy ending to cap the episode. Likely Louie's over-the-top reaction to finally getting his kiss.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Alright, Louie, I'll give you that kiss.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: You will?

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Yes.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: *leans in eagerly*

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: *kisses him briefly, then pulls away*

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: *stumbles backward, dazed, trips over chair*

tmdb_overview: Elaine endures a date with Louie to end the cabbies' strike.
insight: A lean 35-joke episode relies on character friction and escalating awkwardness rather than density.
